threats.title: Arbitrary Caller Can Steal NFT During Isolate Liquidation
threats.hypothesis_description: The isolate liquidation function transfers the liquidated NFT to the caller instead of the highest bidder, allowing anyone to steal the NFT using the highest bidder's escrowed funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function `executeIsolateLiquidate` uses `params.msgSender` as the recipient for the NFT transfer, but uses `loanData.bidAmount` (which belongs to `loanData.lastBidder`) to repay the debt. Since the function is permissionless after the auction ends, an attacker can simply call it to receive the NFT for free.
preconditions: An isolate loan auction has ended (block.timestamp > auctionEndTimestamp)
The attacker calls isolateLiquidate before the legitimate highest bidder

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: NFT ownership is granted to the transaction sender rather than the recorded highest bidder.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time after an isolate auction concludes.

operational_questions: Can the highest bidder prevent this by front-running?
Does the protocol have an off-chain keeper that is expected to call this?
validation_requirements: Test isolate liquidation with a third-party caller account

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor mempool or chain state for isolate auctions that have passed their `auctionEndTimestamp`.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: The protocol uses the highest bidder's funds to clear the debt and transfers the NFT to the attacker.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker successfully acquires the NFT without spending any capital.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete loss of trust in the isolate auction mechanism; liquidators lose funds and receive nothing.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Requires hard fork or manual compensation to affected liquidators.

investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Bots automatically calling liquidate on expired auctions.

threats.title: Caller Can Reduce Their Own Debt by Redeeming Others' Isolate Loans
threats.hypothesis_description: The isolate redeem function incorrectly decreases the borrow balance of the caller instead of the actual NFT owner, allowing an attacker to pay off their own debt using another user's auctioned loan state.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `executeIsolateRedeem`, `VaultLogic.erc20DecreaseIsolateScaledBorrow` is called with `params.msgSender` instead of `tokenData.owner`. If an attacker with an existing isolate borrow calls redeem on someone else's auctioned NFT, the attacker's debt is reduced. The original borrower's aggregate debt remains inflated, corrupting protocol accounting.
preconditions: Attacker has an active isolate borrow in the same group.
Another user has an isolate loan currently in auction.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Debt reduction is applied to `msg.sender` without verifying they are the loan owner.

operational_questions: Does the original borrower's debt remain permanently unpayable?
Can this be used to underflow the attacker's debt?
validation_requirements: Test redeeming a third-party loan and check both users' `userScaledIsolateBorrow` balances.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker creates an isolate loan to establish a `userScaledIsolateBorrow` balance.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker waits for another user's loan (in the same group) to enter auction.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker calls `isolateRedeem` on the victim's loan.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker's debt is reduced, while the victim's loan state is reset but their aggregate debt remains inflated.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Severe accounting corruption; victim users cannot fully repay their phantom debt.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires manual state correction via upgrades.

investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users unable to withdraw NFTs due to phantom debt.
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Targeted redeeming of specific loans by unrelated accounts.

threats.title: Isolate Liquidation Fails to Clear Locker Address, Permanently Bricking Re-Deposited NFTs
threats.hypothesis_description: When an isolate loan is liquidated, the protocol fails to reset the NFT's `lockerAddr`. If the liquidated NFT is ever re-deposited into the protocol, it becomes permanently stuck and cannot be withdrawn.
threats.investigation_rationale: `executeIsolateLiquidate` deletes `loanData` and clears the owner, but does not reset `tokenData.lockerAddr` to `address(0)`. If the NFT is re-deposited, `validateWithdrawERC721` will revert because it strictly requires `lockerAddr == address(0)` for all withdrawals.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Clears owner and supplyMode but omits tokenData.lockerAddr = address(0)

preconditions: An NFT is liquidated via isolate margin.
The same NFT is later re-deposited into the protocol.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Incomplete state cleanup during liquidation leaves residual lock flags.

operational_questions: Can the locker address be manually cleared by an admin?
Does this affect cross-margin deposits of the same NFT?
validation_requirements: Liquidate an isolate loan, re-deposit the NFT, and attempt to withdraw it.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Not an active attack, but a severe operational failure.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Liquidator receives NFT and sells it on the open market.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Unsuspecting user buys the NFT and deposits it into BendDAO V2.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: User attempts to withdraw the NFT and transaction permanently reverts.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent loss of user assets (NFTs stuck in protocol).
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires contract upgrade to clear the locker addresses.

threats.title: Flash Loan Callback Reentrancy Enables Interest Rate Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: The ERC20 flash loan function lacks a reentrancy guard and does not update `availableLiquidity` during the callback. An attacker can reenter the protocol to borrow assets at artificially low interest rates.
threats.investigation_rationale: Because `availableLiquidity` includes the flash-loaned amount during the callback, the utilization rate calculated in `updateInterestRates` is artificially low. An attacker can borrow during the callback, locking in a lower interest rate than market conditions warrant.
preconditions: Flash loans are enabled for the target asset.
Attacker has sufficient collateral to borrow.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Explicit removal of reentrancy guard combined with stale liquidity accounting.

operational_questions: How much can the interest rate be manipulated?
Can this be combined with oracle manipulation?
validation_requirements: Execute a flash loan and reenter `crossBorrowERC20` to observe the recorded `borrowRate`.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker initiates a massive flash loan for Asset X.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: During the callback, attacker calls `crossBorrowERC20` for Asset X.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Protocol calculates utilization using the inflated `availableLiquidity`, resulting in a low rate.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker repays flash loan, keeping the low-interest borrow position.
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Custom smart contract to handle the callback.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Lenders receive less yield than expected; protocol interest rate models are bypassed.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - rates will correct on next interaction, but attacker's rate is locked until then.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Flash loans followed immediately by borrows in the same transaction.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sudden drops in borrow rates despite high actual utilization.

threats.title: Native ETH Withdrawals Revert or Steal Caller's Existing WETH
threats.hypothesis_description: When withdrawing native ETH, the protocol sends withdrawn WETH to the receiver, but then attempts to pull WETH from the caller to unwrap it. This either reverts or burns the caller's personal WETH balance.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `BVault.withdrawERC20`, `executeWithdrawERC20` transfers WETH to `receiver`. Then `unwrapNativeTokenInWallet` calls `transferFrom(user, address(this), amount)` where `user` is `msgSender`. This requires the caller to have approved WETH and uses their personal balance, not the withdrawn funds.
preconditions: User attempts to withdraw, borrow, or liquidate using native ETH.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Unwrapping logic pulls funds from the user instead of intercepting the outbound transfer.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Always active for native token flows.

operational_questions: Does this completely break the native ETH integration?
Can a user accidentally burn their own WETH if they have an active approval?
validation_requirements: Test `withdrawERC20` with native ETH and observe the revert or WETH deduction.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Not an attack, but a severe logic flaw.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Protocol sends WETH to receiver, then tries to pull WETH from the user.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Transaction reverts due to lack of allowance, or user loses their own WETH.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Native ETH flows are completely broken; potential loss of user funds if approvals exist.
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All native ETH withdrawals, borrows, and liquidations.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: High revert rate on `withdrawERC20` with native ETH.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users complaining about missing WETH after ETH withdrawals.

threats.title: Native ETH Deposits Revert Due to Incorrect WETH Transfer Flow
threats.hypothesis_description: Depositing native ETH reverts because the protocol wraps the ETH, sends it to the user, and immediately tries to pull it back using `transferFrom`, which requires a pre-existing WETH approval.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `BVault.depositERC20`, `wrapNativeTokenInWallet` sends WETH to the user. `executeDepositERC20` then calls `erc20TransferInLiquidity`, which uses `safeTransferFrom` to pull the WETH from the user. Since users depositing ETH won't have approved WETH, the transaction reverts.
preconditions: User deposits or repays using native ETH without prior WETH approval.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Wrapping logic sends tokens to the user instead of keeping them in the pool for the subsequent logic.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Always active for native token flows.

operational_questions: Is there any frontend workaround being used (e.g., forcing WETH approvals)?
Does this affect all native token entry points?
validation_requirements: Test `depositERC20` with native ETH from an account with 0 WETH allowance.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Not an attack, but a severe logic flaw.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Protocol wraps ETH and sends WETH to user.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Protocol tries to pull WETH from user, reverting due to lack of allowance.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Native ETH deposits and repayments are broken for standard users.
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All native ETH deposits and repayments.

threats.title: Rounding Errors in rayDiv Cause Full Debt Repayments and Liquidations to Revert
threats.hypothesis_description: When fully repaying a group debt, rounding errors between `rayMul` and `rayDiv` can cause the calculated scaled amount to exceed the user's actual scaled balance by 1 wei, resulting in an underflow revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: `_repayUserERC20Debt` calculates exact debt using `rayMul`. `erc20DecreaseCrossBorrow` converts this back using `rayDiv`. Due to half-up rounding, `amountScaled` can be `originalScaled + 1`. Subtracting this from `userScaledCrossBorrow` causes an underflow, blocking liquidations and full repayments.
preconditions: A user attempts to fully repay their debt, or a liquidator attempts to fully liquidate a group debt.
The specific combination of scaled balance and borrow index causes `rayDiv(rayMul(x))` to round up to `x + 1`.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Lack of a 'full repayment' mitigation (e.g., checking if amount == fullDebt) when converting back to scaled amounts.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Randomly occurs based on interest index values.

operational_questions: How frequently does this rounding error occur in practice?
Can an attacker intentionally manipulate their debt size to trigger this and avoid liquidation?
validation_requirements: Fuzz test `rayDiv(rayMul(x, index), index)` to find values where result > x, and simulate repayment.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker calculates a specific borrow amount that will trigger the rounding error at a future target index.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: When the position becomes liquidatable, liquidators attempt to clear the debt.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: The liquidation transaction reverts due to underflow, protecting the attacker from liquidation.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Positions become temporarily or permanently unliquidatable; normal users cannot fully repay debt.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires users to repay slightly less than 100%.

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: High - requires precise timing and math.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reverted liquidation or repayment transactions with arithmetic underflow errors.

threats.title: ERC721 Flash Loans Bypass Yield Manager Locks
threats.hypothesis_description: The ERC721 flash loan function fails to check if an NFT is locked by a yield manager, allowing users to flash loan locked NFTs out of the protocol.
threats.investigation_rationale: `executeFlashLoanERC721` checks `loanStatus == 0` but omits the `tokenData.lockerAddr == address(0)` check. If an NFT is staked in an external yield protocol (e.g., Lido) via the Yield module, the owner can still flash loan it, potentially breaking invariants in the yield protocol or claiming unauthorized airdrops.
preconditions: An NFT is deposited and locked by a yield manager (`lockerAddr != address(0)`).
Flash loans are enabled for the NFT asset.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Incomplete validation of NFT state before allowing it to leave the protocol.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: While an NFT is locked for yield.

operational_questions: What specific actions can be performed on the NFT during the flash loan callback that harm the yield manager?
Can this be used to unstake the NFT externally?
validation_requirements: Lock an NFT using the Yield module, then attempt to flash loan it.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker deposits NFT and stakes it via the Yield module.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker calls `flashLoanERC721` for the staked NFT.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: During the callback, attacker interacts with the external yield protocol (e.g., claiming rewards or unstaking) using the physical NFT.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker returns the NFT to the pool, leaving the Yield module's state out of sync with reality.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Yield module state corruption; potential theft of yield rewards.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires manual reconciliation of yield states.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Flash loans of NFTs that are currently staked.

threats.title: Missing Oracle Price Validation Allows Zero-Price Mass Liquidations
threats.hypothesis_description: The protocol does not validate oracle prices for zero values. If an oracle returns 0 for a collateral asset, the protocol calculates the user's collateral value as 0 without reverting, immediately making the position liquidatable.
threats.investigation_rationale: In `GenericLogic.calculateUserAccountData`, `assetPrice` is multiplied by the balance. If `assetPrice == 0`, `userBalanceInBaseCurrency` becomes 0. This drops the health factor below 1.0. A temporary oracle failure or manipulation returning 0 can trigger unwarranted mass liquidations.
deployment_assumptions: Oracle can return 0 during extreme market events or failures.
threats.config_evidence: DeployPoolFull.s.sol configures PriceOracle, which relies on external Chainlink aggregators that may return 0 or stale data if unvalidated.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No check for vars.assetPrice == 0 before using it in multiplication.

preconditions: Oracle returns 0 for a collateral asset.
Liquidator bots are monitoring the mempool.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Implicit trust in oracle data without standard sanity checks (e.g., > 0, staleness).

operational_questions: Does the `PriceOracle` contract implement zero checks internally?
Can an attacker force the oracle to return 0 via flash loans?
validation_requirements: Review `PriceOracle.sol` (not provided) for zero checks. If absent, simulate a 0 price return and trigger liquidation.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Oracle experiences a failure or is manipulated to return 0 for Asset X.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: All users using Asset X as collateral instantly have a Health Factor < 1.0.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker seizes collateral at a massive discount.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Mass unwarranted liquidations; severe loss of user funds.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Very High - irreversible loss of user collateral.

execution_requirements_analysis.coordination_complexity: High - relies on external oracle state.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Sudden spikes in liquidations across multiple accounts.

threats.title: Isolate Auctions Can Be Permanently DoS'd if Highest Bidder is Blocklisted
threats.hypothesis_description: In isolate auctions, placing a new bid requires refunding the previous highest bidder via `safeTransfer`. If the debt asset has a blocklist (e.g., USDC) and the highest bidder is blocklisted, the refund reverts, permanently freezing the auction.
threats.investigation_rationale: `executeIsolateAuction` calls `erc20TransferOutBidAmount` to refund `oldLastBidder`. If this transfer fails, the entire transaction reverts. An attacker could intentionally bid using a disposable address and then get it blocklisted, or a legitimate user might be blocklisted mid-auction.
deployment_assumptions: Debt asset is a blocklistable token like USDC or USDT.
threats.config_evidence: InitConfigPool.s.sol adds USDT as an asset, which has a known blocklist mechanism.
preconditions: Debt asset is blocklistable (e.g., USDT).
Highest bidder is added to the blocklist during the auction.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Push-based refund pattern in an auction mechanism.

operational_questions: Can the protocol admin forcefully clear the auction?
Is there a pull-based refund fallback?
validation_requirements: Simulate a USDT blocklist event on the highest bidder and attempt to place a new bid.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker places a bid on an isolate auction using an address they control.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker intentionally triggers a blocklist event on that address (e.g., interacting with sanctioned entities).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Any subsequent bids revert because the protocol cannot refund the attacker.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Auction is permanently frozen; NFT is trapped.
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Ability to get an address blocklisted.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: NFTs permanently trapped in auction state; bad debt cannot be cleared.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires contract upgrade to implement pull-based refunds.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reverted `isolateAuction` calls with ERC20 transfer errors.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Auctions stuck past their end duration.

threats.title: Strict Balance Checks Break Documented Support for Fee-on-Transfer Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: Despite documentation claiming support for fee-on-transfer tokens, the protocol enforces strict pre/post balance equality checks during deposits, causing all interactions with such tokens to revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: `erc20TransferInLiquidity` requires `poolSizeAfter == (poolSizeBefore + amount)`. If a fee-on-transfer token is used, the actual received amount is less than `amount`, triggering the `INVALID_TRANSFER_AMOUNT` revert. This breaks core functionality for these tokens.
deployment_assumptions: A fee-on-transfer token is configured as an active asset.
threats.config_evidence: Project documentation explicitly states: 'ERC20 (Any implementation, explicitly including weird traits: fee-on-transfer...)'
preconditions: A fee-on-transfer token is added to the pool.
A user attempts to deposit or repay using this token.

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Contradiction between documented intent and strict code enforcement.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Admin must add a fee-on-transfer token.

operational_questions: Are there any fee-on-transfer tokens currently planned for deployment?
Does this also affect tokens that deduct fees on transfer out?
validation_requirements: Deploy a mock fee-on-transfer token and attempt `depositERC20`.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Not an attack, but a functional failure.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: User attempts to deposit a supported fee-on-transfer token.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Transaction reverts due to balance mismatch.
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Token is unusable within the protocol.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete inability to use fee-on-transfer tokens.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium - requires contract upgrade to use `balanceAfter - balanceBefore` pattern.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: 100% revert rate for specific token deposits.
